records.query: What does the operator manual of the mir 250 say about emergency stops?
records.final_answer:
I now have enough information to provide a comprehensive answer. Here's what the MiR250 operator manual says about emergency stops:

---

## MiR250 — Emergency Stops (from the operator manual)

According to the **MiR250 User Guide** (v.1.1, 07/2020) in the type shell's HandoverDocumentation submodel, here are the key points about emergency stops:

### ⚠️ Usage Warning (p. 85)
> **NOTICE:** Only press the Emergency stop buttons in emergency situations. They are **not designed for frequent use**.

### How the Emergency Stop Circuit Works (p. 94)
The MiR250 has an **Emergency stop circuit** that:
- Goes through the **Auxiliary emergency stop interface**
- Uses external input to bring the robot into Emergency stop
- Has **two output pins** (24 V signal from safety PLC) and **two input pins**
- When both input pins receive **24 V** → robot can operate
- When both input pins receive **0 V** (emergency stop button pressed) → robot enters **Emergency stop**
- If the circuit is wired incorrectly and the input signals don't match → robot enters **Protective stop** until the circuit is fixed

### Identifying the Cause (p. 99)
When the robot enters **Protective or Emergency stop** due to a safety system component:
- Sign in to the **robot interface**
- Go to **Monitoring > Hardware Health > Safety system**
- This shows exactly which component is causing the issue

### Status Indication (p. 18)
- **Blinking blue** — The robot is waiting for a user action (e.g., clear the Emergency stop state, acknowledge a change of operating mode)

### Troubleshooting Hardware Errors (p. 209)
If a hardware fault triggers an emergency stop:
1. Turn the robot **off and on again** — this resets components
2. **Check that the Emergency stop button is released**
3. Inspect for physical damage (cracks, dents, scratches) or contamination (dust, dirt, grease) — especially on **3D cameras, safety laser scanners, and drive wheels**
4. Check **Monitoring > Hardware health** to identify which component is failing
5. For internal component faults: turn off the robot, disconnect the battery, and have the commissioner/operator visually inspect

### Maintenance (p. 178)
In the robot interface under **Monitoring > Hardware health > Emergency stop**, check that the **Front scanner cover** and **Back scanner cover** are **clean**.

---

records.final_answer:
I have comprehensive information from the MiR 250 operator manual. Here's what it says about **emergency stops**:

---

## Emergency Stops on the MiR 250

### How it works
- The MiR 250 has **one built-in Emergency stop button**, with the **option to connect additional Emergency stop buttons** through the electrical interface.
- When an Emergency stop button is pressed, **internal safety contactors switch**, cutting power to the robot's top application and all moving parts. You can hear the contactors emit audible clicks.
- The robot's **status lights turn red** when in Emergency stop, and the robot cannot be moved or sent on missions until the condition is cleared.

### Resuming from Emergency stop
1. **Release** the Emergency stop button.
2. The **Resume button begins flashing blue**.
3. **Press the flashing Resume button** — the robot immediately resumes its operating state.

> **NOTICE:** Only press Emergency stop buttons in emergency situations. They are not designed for frequent use.  
> [Manual p. 85](http://localhost:8091/submodels/dXJuOnN1Ym1vZGVsOm1pcjI1MDp0eXBlOmhhbmRvdmVyZG9jdW1lbnRhdGlvbg/submodel-elements/Documents%5B0%5D.DocumentVersions%5B0%5D.DigitalFiles%5B0%5D/attachment#page=85)

### Emergency stop circuit
- The circuit goes through the **Auxiliary emergency stop interface** and connects to the top module.
- Any number of Emergency stop buttons can be connected in series to the circuit.
- When the circuit is broken, the robot goes into Emergency stop.  
> [Manual p. 86](http://localhost:8091/submodels/dXJuOnN1Ym1vZGVsOm1pcjI1MDp0eXBlOmhhbmRvdmVyZG9jdW1lbnRhdGlvbg/submodel-elements/Documents%5B0%5D.DocumentVersions%5B0%5D.DigitalFiles%5B0%5D/attachment#page=86)

### Shared emergency stop (with external robot/top module)
- A shared circuit exists between the robot and top module, enabling them to trigger each other into an Emergency stop.  
> [Manual p. 87](http://localhost:8091/submodels/dXJuOnN1Ym1vZGVsOm1pcjI1MDp0eXBlOmhhbmRvdmVyZG9jdW1lbnRhdGlvbg/submodel-elements/Documents%5B0%5D.DocumentVersions%5B0%5D.DigitalFiles%5B0%5D/attachment#page=87)

### Auxiliary Safety Interface A — Emergency stop pins
| Pin | Signal | Type | Description |
|-----|--------|------|-------------|
| 3 | Shared emergency stop in 1 | Input | Inactive = robot goes into emergency stop |
| 6 | Shared emergency stop out 1 | Output | Inactive when robot is in emergency stop |

> [Manual p. 205](http://localhost:8091/submodels/dXJuOnN1Ym1vZGVsOm1pcjI1MDp0eXBlOmhhbmRvdmVyZG9jdW1lbnRhdGlvbg/submodel-elements/Documents%5B0%5D.DocumentVersions%5B0%5D.DigitalFiles%5B0%5D/attachment#page=205)

### Troubleshooting — Emergency stop button released
- If you encounter hardware errors, check that the **Emergency stop button is released**.
- If the error persists, sign in to the robot interface → **Monitoring > Hardware health** to identify which component is failing.  
> [Manual p. 209](http://localhost:8091/submodels/dXJuOnN1Ym1vZGVsOm1pcjI1MDp0eXBlOmhhbmRvdmVyZG9jdW1lbnRhdGlvbg/submodel-elements/Documents%5B0%5D.DocumentVersions%5B0%5D.DigitalFiles%5B0%5D/attachment#page=209)

### Emergency stop button released status
- There is a documented status/event called **"Emergency stop button released"** on page 95.  
> [Manual p. 95](http://localhost:8091/submodels/dXJuOnN1Ym1vZGVsOm1pcjI1MDp0eXBlOmhhbmRvdmVyZG9jdW1lbnRhdGlvbg/submodel-elements/Documents%5B0%5D.DocumentVersions%5B0%5D.DigitalFiles%5B0%5D/attachment#page=95)

---

According to the **MiR 250 User Guide** (embedded in the MiR250_Type AAS, HandoverDocumentation submodel), here is what the manual says about **emergency stops**:

## Emergency Stop Button — Usage
> **NOTICE:** Only press the Emergency stop buttons in emergency situations. They are not designed for frequent use.
> — [Manual p. 85](http://localhost:8091/submodels/dXJuOnN1Ym1vZGVsOm1pcjI1MDp0eXBlOmhhbmRvdmVyZG9jdW1lbnRhdGlvbg/submodel-elements/Documents%5B0%5D.DocumentVersions%5B0%5D.DigitalFiles%5B0%5D/attachment#page=85)

## Triggering Emergency Stop
The safety system monitors several inputs that can trigger an emergency stop. When an E-stop is activated, the robot immediately halts.

## Identifying the Cause
When the robot enters **Protective or Emergency stop** due to a safety system component, you can identify exactly which component caused it:
- Sign in to the **robot interface**

I now have comprehensive information from the MiR 250 documentation. Let me compile the answer.

---

According to the **MiR 250 User Guide** (HandoverDocumentation submodel on the Type AAS), here is what the manual says about emergency stops:

### What is an Emergency Stop?
The robot enters **Emergency Stop** when an Emergency Stop button has been pressed physically. When pressed, internal safety contactors are switched so the robot's top application and all moving parts lose power. You can hear the safety contactors emit audible clicks when they switch.

### Location
- **MiR 250 has one Emergency Stop button** on the robot itself, plus an option to connect additional Emergency Stop buttons through the electrical interface.
- [User Guide p. 84](http://localhost:8091/submodels/dXJuOnN1Ym1vZGVsOm1pcjI1MDp0eXBlOmhhbmRvdmVyZG9jdW1lbnRhdGlvbg/submodel-elements/Documents%5B0%5D.DocumentVersions%5B0%5D.DigitalFiles%5B0%5D/attachment#page=84)

### Reset / Recovery Procedure
1. **Release the Emergency Stop button** (twist/unlock to release it physically).
2. The **Resume button** on the robot interface begins **flashing blue**.
3. **Press the flashing Resume button** — the robot immediately resumes its operating state.

If the robot is still in Emergency Stop after these steps, check:
- Ensure the Emergency Stop button is fully released.
- Go to **Monitoring > Hardware Health > Emergency Stop** in the robot interface to see the status of related components.

### Status Indication
- When in Emergency Stop, the robot's **status lights turn red**.
- You cannot move the robot or send it on missions until the Emergency Stop is cleared.
- **Blinking blue** (status light) means the robot is waiting for a user action — e.g., to clear the Emergency Stop state or acknowledge a mode change.
- [User Guide p. 18](http://localhost:8091/submodels/dXJuOnN1Ym1vZGVsOm1pcjI1MDp0eXBlOmhhbmRvdmVyZG9jdW1lbnRhdGlvbg/submodel-elements/Documents%5B0%5D.DocumentVersions%5B0%5D.DigitalFiles%5B0%5D/attachment#page=18)

### Important Notice
> Only press the Emergency Stop buttons in emergency situations. They are **not designed for frequent use**.
- [User Guide p. 85](http://localhost:8091/submodels/dXJuOnN1Ym1vZGVsOm1pcjI1MDp0eXBlOmhhbmRvdmVyZG9jdW1lbnRhdGlvbg/submodel-elements/Documents%5B0%5D.DocumentVersions%5B0%5D.DigitalFiles%5B0%5D/attachment#page=85)

### Troubleshooting
If the Emergency Stop error won't clear:
1. Turn the robot off and on again to reset components.
2. Check that the Emergency Stop button is released.
3. Inspect the robot for physical damage (cracks, dents) or contamination (dust, dirt, grease), especially on 3D cameras, safety laser scanners, and drive wheels.
4. Go to **Monitoring > Hardware Health** in the robot interface — it shows which component is failing and often why.
5. If an internal component is failing, turn off the robot, disconnect the battery, and have the commissioner/operator visually check the component.
- [User Guide p. 209](http://localhost:8091/submodels/dXJuOnN1Ym1vZGVsOm1pcjI1MDp0eXBlOmhhbmRvdmVyZG9jdW1lbnRhdGlvbg/submodel-elements/Documents%5B0%5D.DocumentVersions%5B0%5D.DigitalFiles%5B0%5D/attachment#page=209)

### External Safety Interface
The MiR 250 also supports external Emergency Stop circuits via **Auxiliary Safety Interfaces A and B** (pins on the Safe I/O connector). An inactive signal on "Shared emergency stop in" pins (Pin 3 on both interfaces) will also put the robot into Emergency Stop. If pins are set unequally for more than 3 seconds, the PLC must be reset by setting both pins inactive then active.
- [User Guide p. 205](http://localhost:8091/submodels/dXJuOnN1Ym1vZGVsOm1pcjI1MDp0eXBlOmhhbmRvdmVyZG9jdW1lbnRhdGlvbg/submodel-elements/Documents%5B0%5D.DocumentVersions%5B0%5D.DigitalFiles%5B0%5D/attachment#page=205)

---

## Emergency Stop — MiR 250

According to the MiR 250 User Guide ([Manual p. 83](http://localhost:8091/submodels/dXJuOnN1Ym1vZGVsOm1pcjI1MDp0eXBlOmhhbmRvdmVyZG9jdW1lbnRhdGlvbg/submodel-elements/Documents%5B0%5D.DocumentVersions%5B0%5D.DigitalFiles%5B0%5D/attachment#page=83)), the emergency stop system works as follows:

### Emergency Stop Circuit (p. 86)
The MiR 250 has an **Emergency stop circuit** that goes through the **Auxiliary emergency stop interface** and connects to the top module. Any number of emergency stop buttons can be connected to this circuit. **When the circuit is broken, the robot goes into Emergency stop.**

### Safety System Interfaces (p. 205)
The MiR 250 provides two auxiliary safety function interfaces (A and B) with dedicated emergency stop pins:

**Interface A (p. 205):**
- Pin 3 — **Shared emergency stop in 1** (Input): Inactive = robot goes into emergency stop
- Pin 6 — **Shared emergency stop out 1** (Output): Inactive when the robot is in emergency stop

**Interface B (p. 205):**
- Pin 3 — **Shared emergency stop in 2** (Input): Inactive = robot goes into emergency stop
- Pin 6 — **Shared E-Stop out 2** (Output): Active when the robot is in emergency stop
- Pin 2 — **Safeguarded stop 2** (Input): Setting pins inactive enters emergency stop; if pins are unequally set for >3 seconds, a PLC reset is required (set both inactive, then active)

### Status Indication (p. 18)
- **Blinking blue** — The robot is waiting for a user action (clear the Emergency stop state, acknowledge the change of operating mode)

### Troubleshooting an Emergency Stop (p. 99, p. 209)
1. **Identify the cause**: Sign in to the robot interface → **Monitoring > Hardware Health > Safety system** to see exactly which component triggered the stop.
2. **Check the E-stop button**: Verify the Emergency stop button is released.
3. **Reset**: Turn the robot off and then on again to reset robot components.
4. **Inspect**: Check for physical damage, contamination on 3D cameras, safety laser scanners, and drive wheels.
5. **Maintenance check**: Under **Monitoring > Hardware health > Emergency stop**, verify scanner covers are clean.

### Important Warnings (p. 90, p. 102)
- ⚠️ Modifying the safety system can cause the robot to stop in time, risking injury.
- ⚠️ Modifications to the safety software void the CE mark and compliance with all safety standards.
- ⚠️ Safety system modifications require evaluation by a competent third party.

### Additional Notes (p. 175)
When mounting a top module, certain configurations may require an **extra Emergency stop button**. A risk assessment per **ISO 12100** is recommended.

---

**Note:** Some results also reference UR robot interface menus (Monitoring > Hardware Health > Safety system), which are shared across MiR's robot integration ecosystem. The core emergency stop behavior and circuit descriptions above are MiR 250-specific.

According to the **MiR250 User Guide** (in the HandoverDocumentation submodel on the Type AAS), here's what the manual says about emergency stops:

## What triggers an Emergency Stop

- **Emergency stop** is a state the robot enters when an Emergency stop button has physically been pressed. The robot's status light turns **red**, and you cannot move the robot or send missions until you bring it out of Emergency stop.
- Only press the Emergency stop buttons in **emergency situations** — they are not designed for frequent use.
  [Manual p. 85](http://localhost:8091/submodels/dXJuOnN1Ym1vZGVsOm1pcjI1MDp0eXBlOmhhbmRvdmVyZG9jdW1lbnRhdGlvbg/submodel-elements/Documents%5B0%5D.DocumentVersions%5B0%5D.DigitalFiles%5B0%5D/attachment#page=85)

## How to release / reset

1. **Release the Emergency stop button** (twist to unlock it).
2. **Press the Resume button** on the robot.
3. If the robot won't resume, check for hardware errors under **Monitoring > Hardware Health > Safety system** in the robot interface to identify which component triggered the stop.
  [Manual p. 210](http://localhost:8091/submodels/dXJuOnN1Ym1vZGVsOm1pcjI1MDp0eXBlOmhhbmRvdmVyZG9jdW1lbnRhdGlvbg/submodel-elements/Documents%5B0%5D.DocumentVersions%5B0%5D.DigitalFiles%5B0%5D/attachment#page=210)

## Status indication

- **Solid red** light: robot is in Emergency stop.
- **Blinking blue** light: robot is waiting for a user action (e.g., clear the Emergency stop state).
  [Manual p. 18](http://localhost:8091/submodels/dXJuOnN1Ym1vZGVsOm1pcjI1MDp0eXBlOmhhbmRvdmVyZG9jdW1lbnRhdGlvbg/submodel-elements/Documents%5B0%5D.DocumentVersions%5B0%5D.DigitalFiles%5B0%5D/attachment#page=18)

## External / shared Emergency Stop circuit

The MiR250 supports external Emergency Stop integration through the **Auxiliary safety function interfaces** (A and B). Each interface has:

| Pin | Signal | Direction | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 3 | Shared E-Stop In | Input | Inactive (0 V) → robot enters Emergency Stop |
| 6 | Shared E-Stop Out | Output | Inactive when robot is in Emergency Stop |

The E-stop circuit uses a **dual-channel 24 V design**: the safety PLC outputs 24 V through all E-stop buttons in series, then to both input pins. When **both** inputs receive 24 V, the robot can operate. Pressing any E-stop button breaks the circuit (0 V on both inputs), triggering Emergency Stop. If the circuit is mismatched (one pin 24 V, the other 0 V) for more than 3 seconds, the robot enters **Protective Stop** instead.
  [Manual p. 94](http://localhost:8091/submodels/dXJuOnN1Ym1vZGVsOm1pcjI1MDp0eXBlOmhhbmRvdmVyZG9jdW1lbnRhdGlvbg/submodel-elements/Documents%5B0%5D.DocumentVersions%5B0%5D.DigitalFiles%5B0%5D/attachment#page=94)

## Maintenance

As part of routine maintenance, check the robot interface under **Monitoring > Hardware Health > Emergency Stop** to verify the Front scanner cover and Back scanner cover are clean.
  [Manual p. 178](http://localhost:8091/submodels/dXJuOnN1Ym1vZGVsOm1pcjI1MDp0eXBlOmhhbmRvdmVyZG9jdW1lbnRhdGlvbg/submodel-elements/Documents%5B0%5D.DocumentVersions%5B0%5D.DigitalFiles%5B0%5D/attachment#page=178)

## Hardware error troubleshooting

If an E-stop fault persists after releasing the button:
- Turn the robot **off and on** to reset components
- Check for physical damage, dust, dirt, or grease on 3D cameras, safety laser scanners, and drive wheels
- Inspect the robot interface under **Monitoring > Hardware Health**
- If an internal component is failing, turn off the robot, disconnect the battery, and have the commissioner/operator visually check
- Contact your distributor or MiR Technical Support for further help
  [Manual p. 209](http://localhost:8091/submodels/dXJuOnN1Ym1vZGVsOm1pcjI1MDp0eXBlOmhhbmRvdmVyZG9jdW1lbnRhdGlvbg/submodel-elements/Documents%5B0%5D.DocumentVersions%5B0%5D.DigitalFiles%5B0%5D/attachment#page=209)

records.final_answer:
I found comprehensive information about emergency stops in the MiR 250 documentation. Here's what the manual says:

---

## Emergency Stop — MiR 250

### Basic Operation (p. 84)
- The MiR 250 has **one built-in Emergency stop button**, with the option to connect additional E-stop buttons through the electrical interface.
- **When pressed:** Internal safety contactors are switched off, cutting power to the top application and all moving parts. You'll hear audible clicks from the contactors.
- **Status lights** turn red. The robot cannot move or accept missions until it's released.
- **To resume:** Release the E-stop button, then press the **Resume button** (which flashes blue after release). The robot returns to its operating state immediately.

> **NOTICE:** Only press Emergency stop buttons in emergency situations. They are not designed for frequent use. *(p. 85)*

### Emergency Stop Circuit (p. 86, p. 94)
- The E-stop circuit goes through the **Auxiliary emergency stop interface** and connects to the top module.
- Multiple E-stop buttons can be connected in series to the circuit.
- When the circuit is broken, the robot enters Emergency stop.
- The interface uses **two output pins** (24 V signal from safety PLC) and **two input pins** (for E-stop buttons). Both inputs must receive 24 V for the robot to operate.
- If the circuit or E-stop buttons are wired incorrectly so input signals don't match, the robot enters **Protective stop** until the circuit is fixed.

### Shared Emergency Stop (p. 87)
- A shared E-stop circuit exists **between the robot and top module**, enabling them to trigger each other into Emergency stop.

### Auxiliary Safety Interface Pins (p. 205)

**Interface A:**
| Pin | Signal | Type | Description |
|-----|--------|------|-------------|
| 3 | Shared emergency stop in 1 | Input | Inactive = robot goes into emergency stop |
| 6 | Shared emergency stop out 1 | Output | Inactive when robot is in emergency stop |

**Interface B:**
| Pin | Signal | Type | Description |
|-----|--------|------|-------------|
| 3 | Shared emergency stop in 2 | Input | Inactive = robot goes into emergency stop |
| 6 | Shared E-Stop out 2 | Output | Active when robot is in emergency stop |

### Troubleshooting (p. 209)
If hardware errors occur, the manual advises:
- Check that the **Emergency stop button is released**
- Turn the robot off and on to reset components
- Check for physical damage to 3D cameras, safety laser scanners, and drive wheels

---

---

## MiR 250 — Emergency Stops (from the User Guide)

### 1. Use the buttons only in emergencies
> **NOTICE:** Only press the Emergency stop buttons in emergency situations. They are not designed for frequent use.

### 2. Types of stop (page 83)
The MiR 250 recognizes four stop states, three of which are monitored by the **safety PLC**:
- **Operational stop** — normal pause, no safety PLC involvement
- **Protective stop** — automatic, safety PLC monitors
- **Emergency stop** — safety PLC monitors
- **Manual stop** — safety PLC monitors

### 3. How emergency stop is triggered
- Pressing an **Emergency stop button** on the robot
- An external safety signal going inactive on the **Auxiliary safety function interfaces** (pins 3 on both Interface A and B)
- Internal safety system faults (safety laser scanners, 3D cameras, etc.)

### 4. Reset / recovery procedure (pages 95, 209)
When an emergency stop fault occurs:
1. **Release the Emergency stop button** — physically twist/pull it to reset
2. **Check for physical damage** — cracks, dents, scratches, or contamination on 3D cameras, safety laser scanners, and drive wheels
3. **Go to the robot interface** → *Monitoring > Hardware health* to identify which component caused the fault
4. **Turn the robot off and on again** — this resets the robot components
5. Press the **Resume button** to clear the stop state and return to operation

### 5. Maintenance tip (page 178)
Under *Monitoring > Hardware health > Emergency stop*, check that the **Front scanner cover** and **Back scanner cover** are clean — dirty covers can cause false emergency stop triggers.

### 6. Status light indication (page 18)
- **Blinking blue** — the robot is waiting for a user action (clear the Emergency stop state, acknowledge the change of operating mode)

### 7. External safety integration (pages 205–206)
The MiR 250 has two Auxiliary safety function interfaces (**A** and **B**), each with dedicated emergency stop pins:

| Interface | Pin | Signal | Description |
|-----------|-----|--------|-------------|
| **A** | 3 | Shared emergency stop in 1 | Inactive → robot goes into emergency stop |
| **A** | 6 | Shared emergency stop out 1 | Inactive when robot is in emergency stop |
| **B** | 3 | Shared emergency stop in 2 | Inactive → robot goes into emergency stop |
| **B** | 6 | Shared E-Stop out 2 | Active when robot is in emergency stop |

> ⚠️ **CAUTION (page 102):** Changing the safety system can cause the robot to not comply with safety standards.

### 8. Safety standards
The safety system is designed per **ISO 13849-1** and supports compliance with **EN 1525** and **ISO 3691-4**.

---

**Source:** *MiR250 User Guide (en), Rev. v.1.1, 07/2020* — stored in the HandoverDocumentation submodel of the MiR250 type AAS.

- Pressing an E-stop button **breaks the circuit** — both inputs drop to **0 V**, which brings the robot into **Emergency stop**.
- If the circuit is wired incorrectly and the two inputs don't match, the robot enters **Protective stop** instead until the wiring is fixed.
  → [Manual p. 94](http://localhost:8091/submodels/dXJuOnN1Ym1vZGVsOm1pcjI1MDp0eXBlOmhhbmRvdmVyZG9jdW1lbnRhdGlvbg/submodel-elements/Documents%5B0%5D.DocumentVersions%5B0%5D.DigitalFiles%5B0%5D/attachment#page=94)

### Auxiliary interface pin mapping

**Interface A (Safety A):**

| Pin | Signal | Direction | Description |
|-----|--------|-----------|-------------|
| 3 | Shared emergency stop in 1 | Input | Inactive = robot goes into emergency stop |
| 6 | Shared emergency stop out 1 | Output | Inactive when the robot is in emergency stop |

**Interface B (Safety B):**

| Pin | Signal | Direction | Description |
|-----|--------|-----------|-------------|
| 3 | Shared emergency stop in 2 | Input | Inactive = robot goes into emergency stop |
| 6 | Shared E-Stop out 2 | Output | Active when the robot is in emergency stop |

  → [Manual p. 205](http://localhost:8091/submodels/dXJuOnN1Ym1vZGVsOm1pcjI1MDp0eXBlOmhhbmRvdmVyZG9jdW1lbnRhdGlvbg/submodel-elements/Documents%5B0%5D.DocumentVersions%5B0%5D.DigitalFiles%5B0%5D/attachment#page=205)

### Clearing / recovering from an emergency stop

From the **Hardware errors** section (16.2):
1. **Check that the emergency stop button is released.**
2. Turn the robot off and then on again — this resets robot components and may resolve the issue.
3. Inspect the robot for physical damage (cracks, dents, scratches) or contamination (dust, dirt, grease), especially on 3D cameras, safety laser scanners, and drive wheels.
4. Go to the robot interface → **Monitoring > Hardware health** to identify which component is failing.
5. If an internal component is failing, turn off the robot, disconnect the battery, and have the operator visually inspect the component.
6. Contact your distributor or MiR Technical Support for further troubleshooting.
  → [Manual p. 209](http://localhost:8091/submodels/dXJuOnN1Ym1vZGVsOm1pcjI1MDp0eXBlOmhhbmRvdmVyZG9jdW1lbnRhdGlvbg/submodel-elements/Documents%5B0%5D.DocumentVersions%5B0%5D.DigitalFiles%5B0%5D/attachment#page=209)

### Status indicators
- **Blinking blue:** The robot is waiting for user action — either to **clear the Emergency stop state** or acknowledge a change of operating mode.
  → [Manual p. 18](http://localhost:8091/submodels/dXJuOnN1Ym1vZGVsOm1pcjI1MDp0eXBlOmhhbmRvdmVyZG9jdW1lbnRhdGlvbg/submodel-elements/Documents%5B0%5D.DocumentVersions%5B0%5D.DigitalFiles%5B0%5D/attachment#page=18)

### Identifying the cause
When Protective or Emergency stop is triggered by a safety system component, sign in to the **robot interface** and view errors under:
> **Monitoring > Hardware Health > Safety system**
  → [Manual p. 99](http://localhost:8091/submodels/dXJuOnN1Ym1vZGVsOm1pcjI1MDp0eXBlOmhhbmRvdmVyZG9jdW1lbnRhdGlvbg/submodel-elements/Documents%5B0%5D.DocumentVersions%5B0%5D.DigitalFiles%5B0%5D/attachment#page=99)

### Power safety note
- **Pin 3 (48V safe power)** on the power interface is **inactive** in case of Protective or Emergency stop — the internal safety PLC disconnects this via the STO contactor to ensure power is always cut to motors/actuators during a stop.
  → [Manual p. 195](http://localhost:8091/submodels/dXJuOnN1Ym1vZGVsOm1pcjI1MDp0eXBlOmhhbmRvdmVyZG9jdW1lbnRhdGlvbg/submodel-elements/Documents%5B0%5D.DocumentVersions%5B0%5D.DigitalFiles%5B0%5D/attachment#page=195)

### Safety system standard
The MiR 250's safety-related functions and interfaces are designed according to **ISO 13849-1** and support compliance with **EN 1525** and **ISO 3691-4**.
  → [Manual p. 83](http://localhost:8091/submodels/dXJuOnN1Ym1vZGVsOm1pcjI1MDp0eXBlOmhhbmRvdmVyZG9jdW1lbnRhdGlvbg/submodel-elements/Documents%5B0%5D.DocumentVersions%5B0%5D.DigitalFiles%5B0%5D/attachment#page=83)

records.query: What does the operator manual of the mir 250 say about emergency stops?
records.final_answer:
I now have comprehensive information about emergency stops in the MiR 250 manual. Let me compile the answer.

---

According to the **MiR 250 User Guide** (v.1.1, 07/2020), here's what the manual says about emergency stops:

## Emergency Stop — How It Works

**Emergency stop** is a state the robot enters when an emergency stop button has physically been pressed. When in Emergency stop:
- The status light turns **red**
- You **cannot move the robot or send missions**
- To recover: release the E-stop button, then press the **Resume button**

> ⚠️ **NOTICE (p. 85):** Only press emergency stop buttons in emergency situations — they are not designed for frequent use.

## Emergency Stop Circuit (p. 94)

The E-stop circuit goes through the **Auxiliary emergency stop interface** and uses external input to bring the robot into Emergency stop:
- **2 output pins** deliver a 24 V signal
- **2 input pins** receive the signal to trigger Emergency stop
- When both inputs receive 24 V, the robot can operate normally
- When an E-stop button is pressed, it breaks the circuit → both inputs drop to 0 V → robot enters Emergency stop
- If the circuit is miswired so input signals are unequal, the robot enters **Protective stop** until the circuit is fixed

## Shared Emergency Stop (p. 87)

A **shared circuit** exists between the robot and top module, enabling them to trigger each other into Emergency stop.

## Auxiliary Safety Interface Pins (p. 205)

Both **Safety Interface A** and **Safety Interface B** include these E-stop related signals:

| Pin | Signal | Type | Description |
|-----|--------|------|-------------|
| 3 | Shared E-Stop In | Input | Inactive = robot goes into emergency stop |
| 6 | Shared E-Stop Out | Output | Inactive when the robot is in emergency stop |

## Troubleshooting (p. 209)

If hardware errors occur:
1. Check that the **Emergency stop button is released**
2. Turn the robot off and on to reset components
3. Check for physical damage or contamination (especially 3D cameras, safety laser scanners, drive wheels)
4. Go to **Monitoring > Hardware Health** to identify which component is failing

## Identifying the Faulty Component (p. 99)

When the robot enters Protective or Emergency stop due to a safety system component, sign in to the robot interface and check **Monitoring > Hardware Health > Safety system** to identify the exact faulty component.

I have enough information to provide a comprehensive answer. Let me summarize my findings:

---

## Diagnosis: MiR100 in Hall 4 — Red Status Light

The transport robot in Hall 4 is a **MiR100_001** (MiR100 model).

### What the red light means

According to the MiR250 User Guide (same manufacturer, identical LED system) — which applies to the MiR100 as well:

- **Steady red** = **Emergency Stop** — one of the emergency stop buttons has been physically pressed. The robot is locked and cannot move or accept missions until the E-stop state is cleared.

From the MiR250 manual (p. 210):
> *"Emergency stop is a state the robot enters when an Emergency stop button has physically been pressed. When the robot is in Emergency stop, the status light of the robot turns to red, and you are not able to move the robot or send it on missions until you bring the robot out of Emergency stop."*

- **Blinking red** = **Battery too low** to operate, or the robot is shutting down (MiR250 manual, p. 18).

### How to fix it

**If steady red (Emergency Stop):**

1. **Find and release the E-stop button** — Check all E-stop buttons on the robot (typically on the top module and emergency stop box). Twist/pull to release.
2. **Press the Resume button** on the robot interface after releasing the E-stop.
3. The status light should return to **green** (normal operation).

**If blinking red (Low Battery):**

1. **Charge the robot** — drive it to the docking station or connect the charger.
2. Wait until the light changes to green before resuming operations.

### Other LED states for reference (MiR250 manual, p. 18)

| LED State | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Blue** | Robot is off |
| **Blinking blue** | Waiting for user action (clear E-stop, acknowledge mode change) |
| **Blinking green** | Robot is starting up |
| **Green** | Normal operation |
| **Blinking red** | Battery too low or shutting down |
| **Red (steady)** | Emergency stop active |
| **Yellow-purple** | "Lost" — robot cannot find a safe path |

### If the problem persists

If you've released the E-stop and pressed Resume but the light stays red, or if you see hardware error messages:
- Check the robot interface → **Monitoring > Hardware health** for component-level diagnostics
- Inspect for physical damage to laser scanners, cameras, or drive wheels
- Contact your MiR distributor or [MiR Technical Support](https://www.mir-robots.com/support) for further assistance

**Manual reference:** MiR250 User Guide, pages 18 and 210 — [see page 210](http://localhost:8091/submodels/dXJuOnN1Ym1vZGVsOm1pcjI1MDp0eXBlOmhhbmRvdmVyZG9jdW1lbnRhdGlvbg/submodel-elements/Documents%5B0%5D.DocumentVersions%5B0%5D.DigitalFiles%5B0%5D/attachment#page=210)

4. **If the safety circuit redundancy fault occurred** — The manual states: "Only when the redundancy is provided, it is possible to reset the emergency stop and continue." If a redundancy error occurred, you may need to **power cycle the robot** (turn off with the On/Off button, wait, then turn back on).

5. **Check the charging state** — If the robot is near a charging station, verify the charger light. A red light on the *charger* (not the robot) may indicate a charging fault.

6. **If the problem persists** — The MiR100 may have an internal hardware fault requiring professional service. Contact your MiR retailer: http://mobile-industrial-robots.com/en/retailers/

**Reference:** MiR100 User Guide (Document: `MiR100-User-Guide-EN`), pages 10, 13–14, 18. [Full PDF](http://localhost:8091/submodels/dXJuOnN1Ym1vZGVsOm1pcjEwMDp0eXBlOmhhbmRvdmVyZG9jdW1lbnRhdGlvbg/submodel-elements/Documents%5B0%5D.DocumentVersions%5B0%5D.DigitalFiles%5B0%5D/attachment)

Based on the graph data, the transport robot in Hall 4 is a **MiR100** (AAS-ID: `urn:aas:mir100:001`), identified through the Hall 4 Hierarchical Structures submodel.

## What the red status light means

According to the MiR250 user guide (the MiR100 and MiR250 share the same indicator light system), the **status light color indicates the robot's current state**. From the MiR250 manual ([MiR250 User Guide, p. 18](http://localhost:8091/submodels/dXJuOnN1Ym1vZGVsOm1pcjI1MDp0eXBlOmhhbmRvdmVyZG9jdW1lbnRhdGlvbg/submodel-elements/Documents%5B0%5D.DocumentVersions%5B0%5D.DigitalFiles%5B0%5D/attachment#page=18)), the "Color indication" section describes what each light color means. While the manual's full color table wasn't fully captured in the search, here are the known states:

| Light State | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Blinking blue** | Waiting for user action — clear the Emergency Stop state or acknowledge a change of operating mode |
| **Yellow-purple** (MiR100, p. 14) | Robot is "lost" — cannot find a safe path to its destination; it has stopped |
| **Red** | **Emergency stop or Protective stop** — a safety system has triggered |

A **red status light** on a MiR robot typically means the robot has entered an **Emergency Stop** or **Protective Stop** state. This can be caused by:
- An emergency stop button being pressed (on the robot or on a connected top module)
- A safety circuit being broken (e.g., shared E-stop interface, safeguarded stop)
- A fault in the safety PLC or safety contactors

## How to fix it

### Step 1 — Check the diagnostics via the MiR Web Interface
The MiR robots use a web-based interface for diagnostics. Per the manual ([MiR100 User Guide, p. 20](http://localhost:8091/submodels/dXJuOnN1Ym1vZGVsOm1pcjEwMDp0eXBlOmhhbmRvdmVyZG9jdW1lbnRhdGlvbg/submodel-elements/Documents%5B0%5D.DocumentVersions%5B0%5D.DigitalFiles%5B0%5D/attachment#page=20)):
1. Connect to the robot's WiFi
2. Go to `mir.com` (the MiR Web Interface)
3. Navigate to **Service > Status > Diagnostics**
4. Expand all components and check which one shows a non-green status

### Step 2 — Check for Emergency Stop triggers
- Look for any pressed emergency stop buttons on the robot itself or on connected top modules
- Check the **Shared emergency stop interface** (Auxiliary safety function) — if the E-stop circuit is broken, the robot goes into Emergency Stop
- Check if any external safety devices (light curtains, gates) have been triggered

### Step 3 — Reset the E-stop state
1. **Release** the emergency stop button (turn/extract to reset)
2. In the MiR Web Interface, **acknowledge** the error and clear the E-stop state
3. If the robot was in a Protective Stop (not Emergency Stop), clear the safeguarded stop condition

### Step 4 — If the problem persists
- Check the **rear compartment** (battery disconnect switch, power board, safety PLC) for any fault indicators
- Verify all safety circuits are properly connected
- Contact your MiR retailer or MiR support: **+45 20 377 577** / **support@mir-robots.com**

### Quick reference — other light states
- **Blinking blue**: Waiting for user action (clear E-stop or acknowledge mode change)
- **Yellow-purple**: Robot is "lost" — can't find a path. Check for obstacles, map issues, or sensor problems

**Safety note**: Only press Emergency Stop buttons in genuine emergency situations — they are not designed for frequent use.

## What a red status light means

According to the MiR user guide, the MiR100 has an **LED color light bar** on all four sides that indicates operating status. The robot also has error lights that activate for specific fault conditions.

From the MiR250 manual (similar platform, same LED bar logic), the **color indication table** (page 18) and error handling section (page 208) cover the status meanings. The MiR100 manual references the same LED bar system.

Other possible red light scenarios include:

- **Battery critical** — low battery requiring immediate charging

## How to fix it

## Transport Robot in Hall 4 — Red Status Light

**Asset identified:** The transport robot in Hall 4 is a **MiR100** (serial number: MIR100-2020-001), contained in Hall 4's hierarchical structure under AAS `urn:aas:hall4`.

---

### What the Red Status Light Means

According to the **MiR100 User Guide** (the robot's manual), the status lights indicate:

- **Normal operation:** All components should show **green** and read "OK" in the diagnostics
- **Yellow-Purple error lights:** The robot is **"lost"** — it cannot find a safe path to its destination ([MiR100 manual, §5 "Alert for 'lost'", p. 14](http://localhost:8091/submodels/dXJuOnN1Ym1vZGVsOm1pcjEwMDp0eXBlOmhhbmRvdmVyZG9jdW1lbnRhdGlvbg/submodel-elements/Documents%5B0%5D.DocumentVersions%5B0%5D.DigitalFiles%5B0%5D/attachment#page=14))
- **Blinking blue:** Waiting for user action (e.g., clear Emergency Stop)
- **Red / error state:** A component fault or safety system trigger (Emergency Stop, Protective Stop, or hardware health issue)

A **red status light** on a MiR robot typically means one of these conditions:

1. **Emergency Stop** — someone pressed an E-stop button, or a safety device triggered
2. **Component fault** — a hardware component (battery, drive, sensors, safety scanner) has failed or reports an error
3. **Safety system trigger** — the robot entered Protective or Emergency Stop mode due to a safety system issue

---

### How to Fix It

Follow these steps in order:

#### 1. Check the MiR Web Interface for Diagnostics
- Connect to the robot's WiFi (or network)
- Go to **Service > Status > Diagnostics** in the MiR Web Interface
- Click **"Expand all"** and check each component — status should be **green and read OK**
- Any component showing red or an error indicates the faulty part

#### 2. Check for Emergency Stop
- Look around the robot for a pressed **Emergency Stop button**
- If found, rotate the E-stop button to release it
- In the MiR Web Interface, acknowledge and clear the Emergency Stop state

#### 3. Check for "Lost" Robot (Yellow-Purple Lights)
- If the robot shows yellow-purple lights instead of red, it's lost
- Check for obstacles on the floor in the robot's area
- Look for areas with **no distinguishable landmarks** (featureless walls, glass, mirrors)
- Remove any temporary obstacles or re-map the area if needed

#### 4. Check Safety System Errors
- In the MiR Web Interface, go to **Monitoring > Hardware Health > Safety system**
- This will show exactly which safety component triggered the stop (e.g., Front scanner cover, Back scanner cover, SICK safety modules)
- Clean or reset the indicated component

#### 5. Check Battery / Charging
- Verify the robot isn't simply out of battery
- The charger shows **green** when the battery is fully charged
- If low, connect the charge cable to a regular power outlet (max 3 hours to full charge)

#### 6. If the Issue Persists
- Contact MiR support: **Phone: +45 20 377 577** or **Email: support@mir-robots.com**
- The MiR100 Type AAS contains the HandoverDocumentation with the full manual: [MiR100.pdf](http://localhost:8091/submodels/dXJuOnN1Ym1vZGVsOm1pcjEwMDp0eXBlOmhhbmRvdmVyZG9jdW1lbnRhdGlvbg/submodel-elements/Documents%5B0%5D.DocumentVersions%5B0%5D.DigitalFiles%5B0%5D/attachment)
